author: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

In this book Augustin refutes the second letter which Petilianus wrote to him after having seen the first of Augustin’s earlier books. This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.

title: Chapter 21

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Chapter 21.—24.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But see, when he is reduced to straits in the argument, he again makes an attack on me full of mist and wind, that the calm clearness of the truth may be obscured; and through the extremity of his want he becomes full of resources, shown not in saying what is true, but in unbought empty revilings.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Hold fast, with the keenest attention and utmost perseverance, what he ought to answer,—that is, where means may be found for cleansing the conscience of the recipient when the stains in that of the giver are concealed,—lest possibly the blast of his eloquence should wrest this from your hands, and you in turn should be carried away by the dark tempest of his turgid discourse, so as wholly to fail in seeing whence he has digressed, and to what point he should return; and see where the man can wander, whilst he cannot stand in the matter which he has undertaken.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For see how much he says, through having nothing that he ought to say.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: He says "that I slide in slippery places, but am held up; that I neither destroy nor confirm the objections that I make; that I devise uncertain things in the place of certainty; that I do not permit my readers to believe what is true, but cause them to look with increased suspicion on what is doubtful."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: He says "that I have the accursed talents of the Academic philosopher Carneades."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: He endeavors to insinuate what the Academics think of the falseness or the falsehood of human sensation, showing in this also that he is wholly without knowledge of what he says.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: He declares that "it is said by them that snow is black, whereas it is white; and that silver is black; and that a tower is round, or free from projections, when it is really angular; that an oar is broken in the water, while it is whole."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And all this because, when he had said that "the conscience of him that gives," or "of him that gives in holiness, is what we look for to cleanse the conscience of the recipient," I said in reply, What if the conscience of the giver be hidden from sight, and possibly be stained with guilt?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Here you have his black snow, and black silver, and his tower round instead of angular, and the oar in the water broken while yet whole, in that I suggested a state of the case which might be conceived, and could not really exist, that the conscience of the giver might be hidden from view, and possibly might be stained with guilt!

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Then he continues in the same strain, and cries out: "What is that what if? what is that possibly? except the uncertain and wavering hesitation of one who doubts, of whom your poet says’—

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: ‘What if I now return to those who say, What if the sky should fall?’"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Does he mean that when I said, What if the conscience of the giver be hidden from sight, and possibly be stained with guilt? that it is much the same as if I had said, What if the sky should fall?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: There certainly is the phrase What if, because it is possible that it may be hidden from view, and it is possible that it may not.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For when it is not known what the giver is thinking of, or what crime he has committed, then his conscience is certainly hidden from the view of the recipient; but when his sin is plainly manifest, then it is not hidden.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: I used the expression, And possibly may be stained with guilt, because it is possible that it may be hidden from view and yet be pure; and again, it is possible that it may be hidden from view and be stained with guilt.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: This is the meaning of the What if; this the meaning of the Possibly.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Is this at all like "What if the sky should fall?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: O how often have men been convicted, how often have they confessed themselves that they had consciences stained with guilt and adultery, whilst men were unwittingly baptized by them after they were degraded by the sin subsequently brought to light, and yet the sky did not fall!

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What have we here to do with Pilus and Furius, who defended the cause of injustice against justice?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What have we here to do with the atheist Diagoras, who denied that there was any God, so that he would seem to be the man of whom the prophet spoke beforehand, "The fool hath said in his heart there is no God?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What have we here to do with these?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why were their names brought in, except that they might make a diversion in favor of a man who had nothing to say? that while he is at any rate saying something, though needlessly, about these, the matter in hand may seem to be progressing, and an answer may be supposed to be made to a question which remains without an answer?
